itineraries.title: Bernina Express: From Engadin to Valtellina
itineraries.subtitle: From St. Moritz to Bormio via Bernina Pass, where Swiss Alps meet Lombardy.
itineraries.notes: Bernina Pass remains open year-round, but winter equipment (snow tires or chains) is required from October through May. The Rhaetian Railway, an alternative to driving the St. Moritz-Tirano route, has been a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2008.

visits.text: Less famous than the Louvre but infinitely more intimate: the Segantini Museum in St. Moritz houses the alpine triptych 'Becoming-Being-Passing Away,' which Giovanni Segantini was painting on the Schafberg when peritonitis claimed his life in 1899 at age 41. The museum opened in 1908, nine years after his death, to exhibit the work within the very landscape that inspired it. Closed Mondays; open 11am to 5pm, CHF 15 adults.

The smallest of the four great lakes of the Upper Engadin: Lake St. Moritz sits at 1,768 meters elevation, stretches 1.6 kilometers long and plunges 44 meters deep. In winter it freezes solid and hosts the White Turf, thoroughbred horse races on ice that have been running for decades. Come summer, the Maloja wind propels sailing regattas across its surface. The lake loop takes an hour on foot; the southern shore remains quiet and undeveloped.

activities.text: Take the southern shore of Lake St. Moritz to reach St. Moritz Bad, the side still spared from concrete development: a 3-kilometer round-trip walk with views of Piz Nair (3,057 meters). On your way back, stop at Hanselmann pastry shop in the village center—grab your pastries on the street if the dining room is packed.

practical_info.tip: Arrive before 11am to avoid the queue at Hanselmann.

visits.text: At 2,234 meters elevation, Lago Bianco takes its name from glacial meltwater: milky white in summer, sky blue on clear days, steel gray when clouds gather over Piz Bernina. It's a reservoir built between 1910 and 1911 to power the Rhaetian Railway—18.6 million cubic meters trapped between stones. Arrive early to catch the surface still smooth, before the wind picks up.

Bernina Pass divides waters between two seas: melt flows north toward the Inn River, then the Danube and the Black Sea; south toward the Adda, the Po and the Adriatic. Ospizio Bernina station, at 2,253 meters, marks the highest point of the highest adhesion railway in Europe, a UNESCO site since 2008. If you're traveling by train, get off at Ospizio Bernina to walk to the Lago Bianco dam.

activities.text: Follow Lago Bianco along the eastern shore trail from Ospizio Bernina station: a gravel path between alpine meadows and glacial reflections of Piz Palü. In good weather, plan 45 minutes round-trip to the northern dam; hiking boots recommended, as the shore can be waterlogged in May and June.

practical_info.tip: Bring warm layers even in July—wind blows regularly across the pass.

visits.text: On Plazza da Cumün, alpine silence gives way to the scent of coffee and the murmur of Italian conversations: this isn't German-speaking Switzerland anymore. The Gothic collegiate church of San Vittore with its Romanesque bell tower, the Sant'Anna oratory, and the baroque chapel of Santa Maria Presentata encircle the square. Allow 20 to 30 minutes; push open the door of San Vittore if it's unlocked.

Colorful neoclassical palaces in an alpine village—it's disorienting. The Spaniolenviertel (Spanish Quarter) was built in the 19th century by Poschiavo natives who returned from Spain and Portugal, where they'd prospered as pastry chefs and confectioners. Devon House (Via dei Palazzi, built in 1863) is the best documented. Start from the train station, a 10-minute walk south.

activities.text: Follow Via dei Palazzi from Plazza da Cumün to cross two centuries of Poschiavo in fifteen minutes: patrician houses with stone-slab roofs on one side, neoclassical villas on the other. End by the Poschiavino stream for coffee. If you have another 45 minutes, the Museo Poschiavino (Palazzo Mengotti) traces the epic of emigrant pastry chefs.

practical_info.tip: Museo Poschiavino closes in winter; check hours at valposchiavo.ch.

visits.text: A red train rolls through the streets of Tirano like a tramway, passing within meters of the Sanctuary of Madonna di Tirano—construction began in 1505 on the site of a 1504 Marian apparition. The carved woodwork of the baroque organ (1608) fills the entire back wall. Free entry; avoid mass times if you're here for the architecture.

Palazzo Salis occupies the historic center of Tirano with its handsome facade and wooded gardens; today it hosts exhibitions and cultural events. Via Bernina and the adjacent lanes reveal an intact Lombard center, walkable in 30 to 40 minutes. Aperitivo in the square afterward—Valtellina wines by the glass at several establishments.

activities.text: Take time for an aperitivo in Tirano before moving on: at 429 meters elevation, you've left the alpine atmosphere for Lombardy. If you're traveling by train, watch the Bernina Express depart from the street: the locomotive rolls on the roadway between cars before climbing toward the pass.

practical_info.tip: Bernina Express departs from the street-level platform: spectacular view from Via Stelvio.

visits.text: Bormio deserves a visit beyond ski season: Piazza del Kuerc (14th century) with its Torre della Bajona—whose bell rang the alarm for the entire County during invasions—forms a dense and well-preserved historic center. Look for the dragon-shaped corbels under the Kuerc's gutter. Off-season (June or September), Via Roma is quiet and its shops accessible.

Inside Palazzo de Simoni (17th century, built on the foundations of a medieval castle), the Civic Museum of Bormio traces the history of the 'Magnifica Terra,' the title of the former autonomous County. On the carved wooden portal, spot the De Simoni coat of arms: a monkey holding a branch and an eagle. Open in summer; verify hours on-site.

activities.text: If the Bagni Vecchi stretch the budget (paid entry, thermal baths in use since Roman times), the Terme Libere di Bormio offers a free alternative: follow the Stelvio road for 2 kilometers from the center, park at the bridge over the Frodolfo, walk upstream 200 meters. Water shoes recommended.

practical_info.tip: A swimsuit and water shoes are all you need for Terme Libere; hot water guaranteed even in winter.

seo.h1_title: Bernina Express: From Engadin to Valtellina
seo.intro_paragraph: In two days, this route links St. Moritz to Bormio via Bernina Pass (2,328 meters), inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2008 along with the Rhaetian Railway. You'll pass from the alpine chic of the Engadin to the Lombard soul of the Valtellina, with stops in the bilingual village of Poschiavo and at the foot of the Renaissance basilica in Tirano.
seo.seo_hook: St. Moritz, Bernina Pass, Poschiavo, Tirano and Bormio: 2 days on the UNESCO Bernina Express route, from Engadin to Valtellina.
featured_list: St. Moritz: frozen lake hosting the White Turf and Segantini Museum open since 1908
Lago Bianco: 1911 reservoir at 2,234 meters on the UNESCO Bernina Pass
Poschiavo: Italian-speaking alpine village with a 19th-century Spanish Quarter
Tirano: Renaissance basilica from 1505 that the Bernina Express passes like a tramway
Bormio: medieval historic center and natural thermal baths at the gateway to the Stelvio

places_taglines.CH::sankt-moritz: Where alpine glamour was born in 1864 and winter tourism began.
places_taglines.CH::passo-del-bernina: Where waters divide between the Black Sea and the Adriatic.
places_taglines.CH::poschiavo: An Italian-speaking Swiss village built by pastry chefs who conquered Spain.
places_taglines.IT::tirano: Where a red train rolls through the streets past a Renaissance sanctuary.
places_taglines.IT::bormio: Medieval towers and Roman thermal caves in the heart of Upper Valtellina.

seo_keywords: Swiss Alps to Italy road trip

highlights: Lago Bianco, a reservoir at 2,234 meters at the highest point of the UNESCO Rhaetian Railway
Poschiavo, an Italian-speaking alpine town with a 19th-century Spanish Quarter
Sanctuary of Madonna di Tirano (1505), passed within meters by the Bernina Express
Bormio, natural cave thermal baths in use since Roman times

itineraries.title: Grand Alpine Traverse, 16 days: Grisons to Trieste
itineraries.subtitle: From Saint Moritz to Trieste by car: the Dolomites, Tyrol, and Friuli across the Eastern Alps in 16 days.
itineraries.notes: Carry snow chains from November to April on Dolomite passes (Pordoi, Falzarego) and check conditions before Cortina. The Canazei-Bolzano section backtracks geographically: allow an extra half-day if the passes are closed.

visits.text: Overrated, they say, but Saint Moritz delivers on at least one promise: the view. The Engadin lake in summer, the Corviglia slopes in winter, and that altitude silence that calms everything. The resort sits at 1,856 meters. Avoid January weekends when prices double without the snow getting any better.

At 1,856 meters in the Engadin valley, Saint Moritz became the birthplace of winter tourism: in 1864, hotelier Johannes Badrutt bet that British guests would appreciate the alpine winter. He was right. The Badrutt family still runs a palace hotel here. Visit the frozen lake if you're here in January.

The Engadin air has that transparency that makes the ridges sparkle and gives mountain colors an almost unreal sharpness. By late November, the first snows blanket the valley before tourists arrive. Book early: Saint Moritz hotels fill up by September for ski weeks.

activities.text: You'll walk down to Lake Saint Moritz, following the shore to the Celerina district, a roughly 5 km loop. In winter, the frozen surface sometimes hosts horse races and polo. Waterproof shoes essential from November to March.

practical_info.tip: Free access to the lake circuit. Trail maps available at the Saint Moritz tourist office.

activities.text: If winter finds you here, the Cresta Run, a natural ice luge track with over a century of history, is the resort's strangest attraction. The track only admits Saint Moritz Club members or their guests. Check cresta-run.com for details.

practical_info.tip: The Cresta Run is not open to the general public. White Turf (races on the frozen lake) takes place in February, accessible without registration.

visits.text: World capital of economic forums, Davos also knows how to forget the WEF the rest of the year. Its Parsenn ski slopes rank among the most extensive in eastern Switzerland, and the lake promenade offers tranquility at the opposite end of UN-style debates. Arrive outside January if queues are a problem.

The Kirchner Museum, a glass cube perched on the heights of Davos, houses a major collection of works by Ernst Ludwig Kirchner, the expressionist who settled here from 1917. The Alpine paintings done from this terrace dialogue with the landscape visible through the windows. Closed Mondays.

The Berghotel Schatzalp, a former sanatorium turned mountain hotel at 1,861 meters above Davos, is accessible by cable car from the center. The region's cure establishments inspired Thomas Mann's The Magic Mountain, published in 1924. Hotel lobby open to visitors during the day.

activities.text: You'll walk along the Davosersee in late afternoon, a lake with changing reflections - gold in autumn, white in winter, green in spring. The walk takes 40 minutes. In January, the lake is sometimes skateable - check conditions with the tourist office.

practical_info.tip: The Davosersee is 5 min from the center. Free entry. Ice skates for rent on-site in season.

activities.text: If you come in summer, the Jakobshorn is accessible by cable car from Davos Dorf and opens onto the Dischma and Sertig hiking trails. The walk down takes about 2h. Closed off-season, check hours on davos.ch.

practical_info.tip: Jakobshorn cable car from Davos Dorf. Included in the Davos Klosters Card in season.

visits.text: For over 5,000 years, Chur has carried the label of Switzerland's oldest town - a claim well documented by archaeological digs on the Bischofsberg. The old town has kept its medieval lanes. Climb to the cathedral in the morning: the side light on the Romanesque facade differs from the afternoon.

Chur Cathedral stands as one of the most surprising in the Grisons: its blend of late Romanesque and Gothic results from a multi-century construction project. The interior is more impressive than the modest exterior suggests. Free entry, donations welcome.

The lanes of Chur's old town descend in tiers toward the Rhine plain, paved and narrow, lined with arcaded houses. The signs mix German and Romansh. On weekdays, tourists are rare here: it's the locals' territory.

activities.text: You'll take the Poststrasse to climb back toward the Altstadt and the stairs leading to the cathedral square from the station in 15 minutes on foot. It's the most direct route to reach the medieval lanes. Stop at the Rätisches Museum for regional collections.

practical_info.tip: Chur station is 15 min walk from the historic center. Free parking beyond 200 m from the center.

activities.text: Capital of the Grisons, Chur easily goes unnoticed by tourists rushing to Saint Moritz. Wrong. The Saturday market on the Postplatz and the local specialty - Bündner Nusstorte, the canton's nut tart - alone justify a stop. Plan at least two hours.

practical_info.tip: Market every Saturday morning on the Postplatz. Bakeries closed Sundays.

visits.text: You'll arrive in Lech via the Arlberg road that opens onto a village of a few hundred permanent residents and dozens of four- and five-star hotels. A discreet resort reserved for selective clientele for several decades. Limited parking: arrive early or take the shuttle from Langen.

A luxury village without the usual glitz, Lech manages to stay discreet despite having one of the most selective clienteles in the Alps. The baroque village church is open during the day, often empty. Best period: March, spring snow and more accessible rates.

activities.text: If you're here in winter, the Arlberg domain connected to Saint Anton is one of Austria's largest. In summer, the Rüfikopf accessible by cable car overlooks the Vorarlberg ridges. Check lift schedules, variable by season.

practical_info.tip: The ski bus connects Lech to Zürs and Saint Anton free in season. In summer, the Rüfikopf cable car departs from the village center.

visits.text: Tyroleans say of Innsbruck that it's 'too small for a metropolis, too large for a village' - and they see this as an asset. Capital of the Land of Tyrol, it crosses on foot in twenty minutes while offering museums, an old town, and the Karwendel Alps as backdrop. Arrive by train: the panorama on arrival.

The Goldenes Dachl, a balcony with 2,657 gilded copper tiles, was built in 1500 by Maximilian I to observe tournaments from his imperial box. The excess is intentional: 2,657 tiles for a balcony. The small museum inside is worth the visit, 20 minutes suffice.

The Hofburg in Innsbruck doesn't have the scope of Vienna's, but concentrates in a few rooms the essence of the Habsburgs' presence in Tyrol. The Hall of Giants gathers portraits of several generations of the imperial family. Book in the morning to avoid school groups.

activities.text: You'll walk up the Maria-Theresien-Strasse to the baroque Annasäule column, erected in 1706 to celebrate Tyrol's defense. The pedestrian street is lined with Venetian baroque facades with the Karwendel Alps visible to the north. Step back from the fountain to frame it all.

practical_info.tip: Free access to Maria-Theresien-Strasse and the Annasäule. The Hofburg charges admission, rates on hofburg-innsbruck.at.

activities.text: The smell of Kiachl, Tyrolean fritters fried in lard, wafts from markets in autumn and winter. During the Christkindlmarkt, the old town smells of cinnamon and Glühwein. Outside the holidays, local bakeries serve their specialties from 7am.

practical_info.tip: Innsbruck's Christkindlmarkt runs from late November to early January, on several old town squares.

visits.text: A popular ski resort, Mayrhofen is also worth visiting in summer: the Zillertal landscapes offer some of Austria's most photographed alpine pastures. Outside peak season, the valley villages return to their slow rhythm. Arrive in May or September to avoid crowds.

The milky green waters of the Ziller rush between granite banks covered in moss and rhododendrons. The alpine meadows have that smell of fresh grass and mountain manure that announces high-altitude chalets. Hike to the Ginzling Alm: 2h one-way, marked trail.

Zillertal locals call their glacier 'the Hintertuxer,' like a well-known neighbor. This permanent glacier accessible from Hintertux is skiable year-round, at over 3,000 meters. Plan half a day and check rates on hintertuxer-gletscher.at.

activities.text: You'll reach the Hintertux glacier by driving up the valley from Mayrhofen to Hintertux. The cable car climbs in several sections to over 3,000 meters. Dress warm even in July: at the summit, it's several degrees below zero.

practical_info.tip: Hintertux is 30 km from Mayrhofen. The cable car closes for a few weeks in autumn for maintenance.

activities.text: The walk from Mayrhofen to Finkenberg follows the Zillertal valley floor in 45 minutes and opens access to several high-altitude trails. Starting point for the local via ferrata as well. Best window: late June to mid-September.

practical_info.tip: Marked trails from Mayrhofen center. Free map at the tourist office.

visits.text: The air at the summit of the Hintertuxer Gletscher is thin and dry even in July: you're over 3,000 meters and skiers in shorts rub shoulders with mountaineers in down jackets. The glacier is visibly receding from one season to the next. Visit it before the picture changes again.

The Hintertux glacier is one of the few Alpine glaciers skiable year-round, accessible from Hintertux by cable car in several sections. National ski teams practice their off-season training here. Check conditions on hintertuxer-gletscher.at.

activities.text: If you come in summer, the upper glacier slopes are virtually deserted outside training groups. The climb to the Tuxer Fernerhaus at 3,250 meters takes 45 minutes. Dress warm: even in August, the summit temperature hovers around 0°C.

practical_info.tip: Access from the end of the Hintertux road, 30 km at the end of the Zillertal. Rates on hintertuxer-gletscher.at.

visits.text: You'll cross the Brenner Pass via the A22 Autostrada at 1,374 meters, without really noticing: it's one of the busiest Alpine crossings in Europe, invisible behind highway ramps. Stop to see the two customs posts side by side, witnesses to Schengen's recent history.

A strategic passage since Roman times, the Brennero was the route of medieval armies and 1943 convoys. The border has been erased since Schengen, but traces of the customs post are still visible. The small chapel on the Italian side is worth a five-minute stop.

activities.text: You'll pause at Brennero to observe the Munich-Verona axis that concentrates one of the densest truck traffics in the Alps. The cafeteria on the Italian side, just after the border sign, is authentic in its own way.

practical_info.tip: Italian motorway vignette required from the border. Buy online before departure.

visits.text: Bressanone has everything of a fine compact medieval capital: cathedral, cloister, episcopal palace, and cobbled lanes, all within a twenty-minute walking tour. The town is mentioned as early as 901 as an episcopal seat, making it the oldest city in Tyrol. Arrive early, before tour buses.

The smell of Speck, ham smoked and dried in the South Tyrolean Alpine wind, fills the artisan butcher shops in the center. The ochre-yellow arcades of the Portici Maggiori create a typically Mediterranean play of light. Enter a Weinstube for a Speck platter and a glass of local Lagrein.

Bressanone locals will direct you to the Augustinian abbey of Novacella, 3 km north, for its white wines and 12th-century cloister. The tasting cellar is open in the afternoon. Take the bike path along the Isarco to get there in 15 minutes.

activities.text: You'll cross the Portici Maggiori to Bressanone cathedral and its well-preserved Romanesque cloister. The cloister visit takes 30 minutes and is often quieter than the nave. The neighboring Palazzo Vescovile completes the visit.

practical_info.tip: The cathedral is free. The Palazzo Vescovile (diocesan museum) charges admission, about 6 EUR.

activities.text: If abbey wines interest you, Novacella produces Sylvaner and Kerner from high altitude recognized throughout northern Italy. Tasting at the monastery cellar on weekday afternoons. Reservations recommended in July and August.

practical_info.tip: Novacella Abbey (Stift Neustift) 3 km north of Bressanone, accessible by bike along the Isarco. Cellar open Monday to Saturday.

visits.text: You'll climb to the Castello di Brunico, built in the 13th century by the Bishop of Bressanone, which dominates the town from the hill above the shopping center. The view over the Valle della Rienza is clear in tramontane weather. Fifteen-minute walk from the center.

The MMM Ripa, one of Reinhold Messner's six mountain museums, has been installed in the Castello di Brunico since 2011. It's dedicated to mountain peoples of the world: Tuareg, Tibetans, Maasai. 'Ripa' means 'mountain man' in Tibetan. Open May to October, check hours.

activities.text: If the weather is good, the Plan de Corones (Kronplatz) is accessible by cable car from Brunico and offers one of the broadest panoramas of the Dolomites, at 2,275 meters. The platform alone justifies the trip. Check hours: variable by season.

practical_info.tip: The Kronplatz cable car departs from Brunico town. In summer, the summit houses the LUMEN museum dedicated to mountain photography.

visits.text: The water color is unreal: pale emerald against white rocks of the Croda del Becco. Lake Braies (Pragser Wildsee), at 1,496 meters in the Pustertal, is one of the most photographed lakes in the Dolomites. Arrive before 8am in summer: access is regulated and charged after.

Lake Braies is too famous for its own good - and yet the north shore of the lake, beyond the chalet-hotel, stays quiet even in high season. The lake circuit on foot takes 1h30 for 3.5 km. Swimming is discouraged: the water stays icy year-round.

activities.text: You'll rent one of the wooden rowboats from the Pragser Wildsee Hütte dock to approach the vertical walls of the Croda del Becco. Rental lasts 30 to 45 minutes. Arrive before 9am: boats go quickly in high season.

practical_info.tip: Boat rental rates posted on-site. In July-August, car access reserved for hotel guests after 9am: come early or take the shuttle.

visits.text: The Dolomites suddenly tip at Passo Falzarego: on one side the Lagazuoi and its vertical walls, on the other the long descent to Cortina. The enrosadira, the limestone's reddening at sunset, is particularly violent here. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset.

Passo Falzarego, at 2,105 meters, isn't the highest of the Dolomite passes but concentrates the best panoramas. The rifugio terrace at the pass offers coffee with a view of the Tofane. Cable car to the Lagazuoi in season.

activities.text: You'll take the Lagazuoi cable car from the pass to access the Great War trenches carved into the rock at 2,835 meters. The descent on foot through underground galleries takes 1h30. A headlamp is necessary for the tunnels.

practical_info.tip: Lagazuoi cable car at the pass, one-way fare about 10 EUR. Headlamp essential for the 1914-18 underground galleries.

visits.text: Olympic village in 1956 and again in 2026 with Milan, Cortina d'Ampezzo navigates between its past as a winter sports pioneer and Olympic renovation sites. Construction barriers coexist with luxury terraces. Avoid August and international competition weeks.

The Tofane, Cristallo, and Sorapiss surround Cortina with an amphitheater of Dolomite walls whose hues shift from white to red at sunset. This phenomenon, enrosadira in Ladin, is particularly visible from Col Falzarego. Arrive 45 minutes before sunset to find a good spot.

The Corso Italia deserves its reputation: shops, cafes, terraces, and Tyrolean facades in a setting of vertical mountains. As expensive as Saint Moritz here, but more welcoming. Coffee and cornetto in the morning at Bar Sport to see the real clientele.

activities.text: You'll climb to the Croda da Lago refuge via the Falzarego cable car then 2h of walking on a marked trail. The view of the Fanes group from the refuge is striking. Depart before 9am to have the trails to yourself.

practical_info.tip: Col Falzarego is 15 km from Cortina. Paid cable car, check seasonal opening hours.

activities.text: The air of Cortina, at 1,224 meters, has the lightness of mid-altitude Alps. From a rifugio terrace in late afternoon, local Pinot bianco and slices of Speck taste particular. Book rifugi in advance in July-August, often full.

practical_info.tip: Rifugi accessible from Cortina without climbing: Rifugio Faloria (cable car) and Rifugio Son Forca (road accessible).

visits.text: Passo Pordoi, at 2,239 meters between the Sella group and the Marmolada, is the highest road pass on the Great Dolomites Route. The vertical walls of the Sella form an amphitheater to the east. Arrive early: in July, cyclists attack it from 7am.

A high-altitude pass yet accessible by car all summer season, Passo Pordoi resembles nothing else in the Alps. The summit restaurants serve strudels at 2,239 meters. The Terrazza delle Dolomiti at the top of Piz Pordoi alone justifies the stop.

activities.text: You'll climb to Piz Pordoi by cable car (2,952 meters) for a 360-degree view over all the eastern Dolomites. The climb takes 6 minutes from the pass. Closed in bad weather: check on sas.it.

practical_info.tip: Cable car from the pass, round-trip fare about 15 EUR. Bring a jacket: the summit is often windy even in summer.

visits.text: Climbers nickname the Marmolada 'the cathedral of the Alps': at 3,343 meters, it's the roof of the Dolomites and the glacier visible from Canazei recedes year by year. Climb to Punta Rocca early in the morning to avoid afternoon cumulus.

Canazei doesn't look like much, but it's the best base for tackling the Sella Ronda. A valley-bottom village surrounded by Dolomite walls, it concentrates more accommodation than better-known towns. Arrive Sunday evening for weekday rates.

The Sella Ronda is a ski circuit that loops around the Sella massif passing through four passes: Gardena, Campolongo, Sella, and Pordoi. The ski loop takes half a day at a relaxed pace. By bike, the same circuit becomes a several-hour route depending on pace.

activities.text: You'll drive the Passo Pordoi from Canazei, one of the most accessible Dolomite passes, at 2,239 meters. The road climbs in tight switchbacks with views of the Sella group walls. Avoid Sunday mornings in summer: cyclists invade the road at dawn.

practical_info.tip: Passo Pordoi is 14 km from Canazei. The Terrazza delle Dolomiti at the summit offers exceptional panoramas.

activities.text: If you come in summer, the Marmolada cable car from Malga Ciapela (access via Passo Fedaia from Canazei) climbs to Punta Rocca. 360-degree view over the Dolomites. Closed in bad weather: check on marmolada.com.

practical_info.tip: Malga Ciapela is about 35 km from Canazei via Passo Fedaia (2,057 m). Bring a jacket: the Marmolada summit can be cold even in summer.

map_keywords: South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology Bolzano

visits.text: Discovered in the Alpine ice in September 1991, Ötzi - the Similaun iceman - has been displayed since 1998 at the Museo Archeologico dell'Alto Adige. He's about 5,300 years old. Come in the morning, the line is shorter before 11am.

Capital of South Tyrol, Bolzano is sometimes Italian - cuisine, sun, terrazze - and sometimes Germanic - discipline, bilingual signs, beer. This dual identity isn't a problem to manage: it's the charm. The morning market on Piazza delle Erbe is the best illustration.

At the Piazza delle Erbe market, the smell of alpine cheeses and Val Venosta apples mingles with coffee from surrounding bars. Vendors alternate German and Italian depending on the customer. Arrive before 11am for the choice of producers.

activities.text: You'll visit the Museo Archeologico dell'Alto Adige to see Ötzi behind his climate-controlled glass at -6°C. The French audio guide is included with admission. Plan at least 1h30: the contextual exhibition around the iceman is as interesting as the mummy itself.

practical_info.tip: The museum is at Via Museo 43, 5 min walk from Piazza Walther. Closed Mondays. Adult admission about 13 EUR, check on iceman.it.

activities.text: Piazza Walther, named after Walther von der Vogelweide - the medieval troubadour whose statue graces the square - is best in late afternoon, when the Gothic cathedral turns pink. The dispute over the best local Tiramisu is unresolved among competing cafes.

practical_info.tip: Bolzano's Christmas market is one of the oldest in Italy, from late November to early January on Piazza Walther.

visits.text: You'll follow the Adige to reach Trento's Piazza del Duomo from the station, a 10-minute walk past medieval ramparts. The piazza market is lively Saturday mornings. A good introduction before climbing to the castello.

The Castello del Buonconsiglio, residence of the Prince-Bishops of Trento, hosted sessions of the Council of Trent between 1545 and 1563, which reformed Catholicism against the Protestant Reformation. The frescoes of the cycle of Months in the Torre d'Aquila, around 1400, are among the finest in northern Italy. Plan 2h.

activities.text: You'll continue to the MUSE, Trento's science museum designed by Renzo Piano, at the city's edge. The architecture is worth as much as the exhibitions. Open 10am to 6pm except Monday, rates on muse.it.

practical_info.tip: The MUSE is 20 min walk from the historic center, at the end of Corso del Lavoro e della Scienza.

map_keywords: Ponte degli Alpini Bassano del Grappa

visits.text: Palladio designed larger bridges, but none has the lightness of Bassano's Ponte degli Alpini. The wooden structure, based on the master's plans from 1568-69, has been destroyed and rebuilt several times, notably by the Alpini during the Great War. It doesn't look like a monument.

Behind the postcard image of the bridge, Bassano del Grappa hides a heavy military chapter: the Monte Grappa overlooking the town was a World War I battlefield. Two ossuaries at the summit contain the remains of thousands of Italian and Austrian soldiers. A sober and necessary visit.

The Bassanesi call it 'the city of grappa.' The Grapperia Nardini, founded in 1779, has been installed at the foot of the Ponte degli Alpini since its creation. It's a way to understand why the town became the world reference for this spirit.

activities.text: You'll cross the Ponte degli Alpini to reach the Grapperia Nardini, founded in 1779 and considered the oldest in Italy still operating. Tasting is at the counter, standing, like the Bassanesi. No reservation needed.

practical_info.tip: The Grapperia Nardini is at the left foot of the Ponte degli Alpini. Open daily, free tastings with possible purchase.

activities.text: The alcohol of grappa perfumes the cellars and lanes near the bridge. At the Grapperia Nardini, the house serves its Mezzo e Mezzo - half grappa half herbal liqueur - in a crystal glass at the bistro counter. You rarely stay for just one glass.

practical_info.tip: The Poli Museo della Grappa is at Via Gamba 6, 5 min from the bridge. Free entry, paid guided tour.

visits.text: Monte Grappa, at 1,775 meters, was one of the deadliest theaters of the Great War on the northeastern front. The Sacrario Militare at the summit contains the remains of tens of thousands of Italian and Austro-Hungarian soldiers. Access by road from Bassano.

The access road from Bassano climbs in tight hairpins through a beech forest before opening onto the sacrario plateau. The air is several degrees cooler than below. The two ossuaries, one Italian and one Austro-Hungarian, have faced each other since 1935.

activities.text: A military monument yet Monte Grappa is also a popular hiking summit on weekends. The view on clear days embraces the Venetian plain to the Adriatic. The Great War museum at the summit deserves 45 minutes. Open in season, free entry.

practical_info.tip: Access road open May-November. In winter, possible closure after snowfall. Check on montegrappa.it.

visits.text: You'll reach the Rocca di Asolo via the lanes that climb from Piazza Maggiore, about 20 minutes on foot. The view from the ramparts embraces the Veneto hills that poet Carducci called 'the city of a hundred horizons.' Open weekends, check weekday hours.

The 'city of a hundred horizons' deserves its reputation: from the Rocca, the gaze slides over hills covered with vines and campanili. Caterina Cornaro, exiled Queen of Cyprus, chose Asolo for her court in 1489 - understandably. Plan 2h for the old town.

activities.text: You'll stroll on Asolo's Piazza Maggiore before climbing to the Rocca. Thursday morning markets are an opportunity to see producers from neighboring hills sell their products. Arrive hungry: the market cured meats deserve a tasting.

practical_info.tip: Free parking at the village entrance, cars prohibited in the historic center.

visits.text: Valdobbiadene winemakers claim that the Conegliano Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore DOCG hills, UNESCO-listed in 2019, produce something incomparable to industrial plain prosecco. The Cartizze, 107 hectares of grand cru in a pentagon shape on the hill, is the centerpiece.

The Cartizze bubble holds a flavor of white peach and acacia flower, different from ordinary prosecco. Small family cantinas on the heights open their tasting bars on weekends. Avoid the large cooperatives on the main road: prefer the hills.

activities.text: You'll stop at a family cantina in the hills while driving up the Valmareno road. These small producers sell at the cellar, no appointment needed Saturday mornings. A glass of Superiore with a view of the vines beats the best restaurant in town.

practical_info.tip: The Strada del Prosecco starts from Conegliano and climbs to Valdobbiadene: a fully signposted route through the UNESCO hills.

visits.text: Giovanni Battista Cima, called Cima da Conegliano, was born here around 1460 and left his hometown several altarpieces among the most delicate in Venetian painting. His works dot the old town churches. The cathedral houses one of his masterpieces, free visit.

Less known than Venice, yet 60 km away, Conegliano protects a Via XX Settembre lined with Venetian arcades decorated with 15th- and 16th-century frescoes. In September, a grape harvest festival gathers prosecco producers. The rest of the year, tourists are rare here.

activities.text: You'll climb to the castello via the path that starts behind the cathedral, about 15 minutes on foot. View over the Prosecco Superiore DOCG hills on clear days. The museo civico is open Tuesday to Sunday.

practical_info.tip: The castello civic museum charges admission, about 3 EUR. The walk up is worth the trip even if the museum is closed.

visits.text: What a strange town Vittorio Veneto is, born in 1866 from the merger of Ceneda and Serravalle - two distinct towns, two squares, two altitudes, two characters. The Battle of Vittorio Veneto in October 1918 sealed the war's end on the Austro-Hungarian side. A town with a double bottom.

The medieval ruins of Serravalle stand on the hill above the lower town. Saint Mark's lions adorn the Venetian palaces of Piazza Marc'Antonio Flaminio. On summer evenings, residents bring chairs out onto the cobblestones and the piazza stays lively until midnight.

activities.text: You'll visit the Museo della Battaglia at the Castello di San Martino, which traces the 1918 offensive and the collapse of the Austro-Hungarian army. The visit takes 45 minutes. The walk from the lower town offers a fine view over the Venetian plain.

practical_info.tip: The Museo della Battaglia is at the Castello di San Martino, Serravalle (upper town). Closed Mondays.

visits.text: They're simply called the 'mosaicisti': students at the Scuola Mosaicisti del Friuli have restored mosaics at Saint Peter's Basilica in Rome, the Holy Sepulchre dome in Jerusalem, and subway stations in New York. The school accepts weekday visitors by appointment.

The Scuola Mosaicisti del Friuli in Spilimbergo, founded in 1922, is the world's leading mosaic training school. Spilimbergo's duomo itself displays mosaics and bas-reliefs on the Romanesque-Gothic facade. The interior houses remarkable medieval frescoes.

Spilimbergo deserves better than the label 'city of mosaicists': its medieval castello and stone-cut lanes form a harmonious ensemble, rare for a town this size. The view of the Tagliamento from the ramparts alone justifies the stop. Free access.

activities.text: You'll visit Spilimbergo's duomo whose Romanesque-Gothic facade is adorned with medieval mosaics and bas-reliefs. The interior houses medieval frescoes often little mentioned in guides. Free, open daily except midday closure hours.

practical_info.tip: The Scuola Mosaicisti accepts weekday visits by reservation: scuolamosaicistifriuli.it. Town galleries exhibit works by former students.

activities.text: If you pass on a Saturday morning, Spilimbergo's center market gathers Friulian producers: Montasio cheeses, cured meats, wines from neighboring hills. A good opportunity to assemble a picnic before the road to Udine.

practical_info.tip: Market on Saturday morning on Piazza Garibaldi. Off-market, producer shops open during the day.

visits.text: You'll linger at a San Daniele DOP prosciutto producer in the historic center for a tasting. The difference from Parma prosciutto? San Daniele is recognized by its guitar shape and the paw still attached. A direct tasting at the norcineria is enough to verify.

The aroma of prosciutto aged at least 13 months in Friulian air fills the streets of San Daniele's center. This ham has a sweeter flavor than its Parma competitor, according to locals. The Adriatic wind combined with alpine air creates unique drying conditions.

activities.text: Capital of Friulian dry ham, San Daniele del Friuli also hides an unexpected treasure: the Biblioteca Guarneriana, founded in 1466, preserves medieval illuminated manuscripts among the most precious in northeastern Italy. Visit by reservation. Don't enter with a prosciutto sandwich, even if it's local.

practical_info.tip: The Guarneriana is at Via Roma 1. Visits by reservation, free. Aria di Festa (prosciutto festival) in June.

visits.text: First capital of the Lombard duchy in Italy, Cividale del Friuli was founded by Julius Caesar under the name Forum Iulii - which gave the Friuli region its name. The mid-8th century Tempietto Longobardo, UNESCO-listed in 2011, is the best-preserved Lombard monument in Europe.

The Tempietto Longobardo is one of the most intact works of the Lombard world: stuccos, frescoes, and 8th-century mosaics in a room barely larger than a chapel. Morning light streams through a small east-facing window. Come at 9am for this grazing light on the stuccos.

Cividale's Ipogeo Celtico, a network of underground galleries whose origins remain debated, descends several meters beneath the old town. The humidity and smell of stone recall that Cividale's soil has layers. Guided tour only, reservation at the tourist office.

activities.text: You'll cross the Ponte del Diavolo, a medieval bridge over the Natisone, to reach the east bank with views of the cliff and ramparts. Legend says the devil built it for a soul. At night, the bridge is beautifully lit.

practical_info.tip: The Tempietto is at Via Monastero Maggiore, paid entry (~4 EUR). Cividale is 17 km from Udine via the SP56.

activities.text: Known for its Lombard Tempietto, Cividale also hides remarkable Friulian gastronomy: its centro storico osterie serve boreto alla graisana, white fish stew with vinegar and garlic. A local dish little known outside Friuli, ask the owner.

practical_info.tip: Osteria Alla Vedova and Trattoria Al Monastero: two centro storico addresses for boreto. Weekend reservations recommended.

visits.text: Udine is often described as a 'little Venice' - a flattering but inaccurate comparison: it's actually livelier and cheaper than Venice. The Piazza Libertà and its Loggia del Lionello (1448) have rare architectural coherence for an Italian provincial square. Paid parking at the foot of the hill.

Giambattista Tiepolo's frescoes at the Oratorio della Purità deploy their blues and ochres with airy lightness. Tiepolo worked in Udine from 1726 to 1729, at 30, and here he found his style. Open mornings, brief but intense visit.

Friulians say Udine is 'the true capital of Friuli' - administratively, the region has Trieste, but the cultural heart beats here. The Piazza Matteotti morning market offers emblematic products: baccalà mantecato, frico, Montasio. Arrive before 10am.

activities.text: You'll climb via Mercatovecchio to Piazza Libertà from the Loggia. The climb to the castello takes 10 minutes and offers a view over the bell tower and Friuli hills. The castello museum is worth the visit for the Tiepolo collections.

practical_info.tip: The Oratorio della Purità is next to the Duomo, open a few hours in the morning. The castello is 10 min walk from Piazza Libertà.

activities.text: Known for its art collections, Udine also hides one of northern Italy's richest gastronomic traditions. Frico, a potato and melted Montasio cheese pancake, exists nowhere else with this texture. Ask for it at an osteria, not a tourist restaurant.

practical_info.tip: Trattoria da Ivo (Via Grazzano) and Osteria ai Barnabiti: two local addresses recommended for authentic frico.

visits.text: Neither quite Italian, nor anymore Austrian, Trieste has navigated since 1954 between two identities with particular ease. It was the Habsburgs' main port until 1918. Today, it's closer to Ljubljana than Rome in mindset. Arrive by train from Udine to see the sea emerge.

James Joyce settled in Trieste in 1904 to teach English and lived there over a decade. He wrote Dubliners and much of A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man in his Trieste apartments. Several plaques recall his passage on old center streets.

The saline air of the Adriatic invades Piazza Unità d'Italia, the largest square open to the sea in Italy, in late afternoon. The Bora, the northeast wind that can reach impressive speeds, refreshes the city a few days a year. When it blows, the Triestini go out anyway.

activities.text: You'll head down to the Castello di Miramare following the coast from the center, either 8 km on foot or 15 minutes by bus. The castle was built by Archduke Maximilian of Austria between 1856 and 1860. Enter early: groups arrive around 11am.

practical_info.tip: Bus 36 from Piazza Oberdan to Miramare, 15 min, 1.35 EUR. The park around the castle is free, the castle charges admission (~10 EUR).

activities.text: Trieste caffè is a science apart: here, a 'caffè' isn't a simple espresso - a 'nero,' a 'capo,' a 'deca' are codes specific to Trieste. Caffè San Marco, open since 1914, remains the best place to learn the vocabulary.

practical_info.tip: Caffè San Marco is at Via Cesare Battisti 18, 5 min from Piazza Unità. Open daily, closed Tuesday.

seo.h1_title: Grand Alpine Traverse, 16 days: Grisons to Trieste
seo.intro_paragraph: From Saint Moritz to Trieste, this 16-day road trip crosses the Swiss Engadin, Austrian Tyrol, the Dolomites, and Friuli via passes, valleys, and forgotten towns that link the Alps to the Adriatic. Four countries, eleven regions, one logic: always eastward and downward.
seo.seo_hook: From Saint Moritz to Trieste, 16 days across the Engadin, Tyrol, Dolomites, and Friuli: the grand traverse of the Eastern Alps by car.
featured_list: Saint Moritz and the Engadin: birthplace of winter tourism since 1864
Innsbruck: the Goldenes Dachl with 2,657 gilded tiles and the Karwendel Alps
Dolomites: Lake Braies (1,496 m), Olympic Cortina, and the Sella Ronda
Bolzano: Ötzi the iceman (~5,300 years old) at the Archaeological Museum

itineraries.title: Secret Ticino, 3 days: Bellinzona, Verzasca and Vallemaggia
itineraries.subtitle: Drive from Bellinzona to Ascona: medieval castles, turquoise gorges and granite villages in 3 days.
itineraries.notes: The Vallemaggia can be cut off after heavy snowfall—check road conditions in March and April. In August, the Locarno Film Festival drives up prices and crowds throughout the region.

visits.text: Commanding the plain from its rocky spur, Castelgrande is the centerpiece of Bellinzona's three fortresses, inscribed on the UNESCO list in 2000. Its two medieval towers, the Torre Nera and the Torre Bianca, have dominated the valley for centuries. Allow 90 minutes: the archaeological museum is worth the entry fee, but it's the view from the ramparts that justifies the climb.

Less imposing than its neighbor, Castello di Montebello still rewards the ascent: its archaeological museum is surprisingly excellent. What makes Bellinzona unique is the murata, the defensive walls connecting all three castles. Avoid midday in summer—the rock absorbs the heat mercilessly.

activities.text: You'll leave the town center to walk along the murata to Castello di Sasso Corbaro, the third castle often overlooked by tourists in a hurry. The walk takes 30 to 45 minutes one way. The castle houses a permanent exhibition on the history of fortifications. Bring water: there's little shade on the path in summer.

practical_info.tip: Access to the murata is free. The three castles charge separate admission; rates at fortezzabellinzona.ch.

visits.text: The Verzasca's water is such an improbable turquoise green that you instinctively look for the photo filter. The Ponte dei Salti, a double stone arch from the 17th century rebuilt in 1960 after partial destruction, spans the river a few meters above natural pools. Arrive before 10 a.m. in July and August: after that, it's mobbed.

Wild in appearance, the Valle Verzasca is nevertheless dotted with small granite villages inhabited since the Middle Ages. Brione, Gerra, Sonogno: each hamlet has its bell tower and stone alleys. The lanes are deserted off-season, which alone makes the detour worthwhile.

activities.text: If you're visiting between June and September, take a dip in the Verzasca's pools: the water stays cool all summer, often barely above 50°F. The level varies with upstream rainfall, so check before diving in. The rocks are slippery—sports sandals are better than bare feet.

practical_info.tip: Swimming is unsupervised and free. Close supervision essential with children—currents can be locally strong.

visits.text: Sonogno is the end of the world for the Verzasca: twenty or so granite houses pressed against the mountain, a stone bridge, a silence broken only by the torrent and the bells of livestock. The road stops here. Beyond lies only trail.

Verzasca residents say Sonogno resisted rural exodus better than other villages thanks to its two hiking refuges. The small local museum, housed in a granite dwelling, traces life before the automobile. Open in summer, a few hours a day.

activities.text: You'll walk to the granite bridge upstream from the village to see the Verzasca in its final gorges. The round-trip stroll from the square takes 20 minutes. Last coffee available at the village rifugio before heading back down toward Lavertezzo.

practical_info.tip: Sonogno is 40 minutes' drive from Locarno via Gordola. No gas station in the valley: fill up beforehand.

visits.text: Hard to imagine a grander, more beautiful Italian piazza... that happens to be in Switzerland. Locarno's Piazza Grande hosts 8,000 spectators on its open-air grandstands each August for the film festival, founded in 1946. Arrive on a weekday outside festival season: you can settle onto a terrace without jostling.

Ticino residents simply call it 'la Madonna': the sanctuary of Madonna del Sasso has overlooked Lake Maggiore since 1480, when the Franciscan friar Bartolomeo d'Ivrea claimed to have had a vision of the Virgin. The original chapel remains visible, surrounded by the stations of the cross. Arrive early in the morning to avoid tour groups.

Beneath the arcades of the old town, the scent of flowers mingles with that of coffee: here you're in Switzerland, but everything feels like Italy. The lanes around Castello Visconteo lead to small shaded squares and artisan shops. The castle is often closed on Mondays—check hours before climbing up.

activities.text: You'll take the funicular from the bottom of Via al Sasso: 3 minutes climbing the rock face to reach the Madonna del Sasso sanctuary. The fare includes the round trip. The descent on foot via the stations of the cross takes 20 minutes and offers fine views of the lake.

practical_info.tip: Departure from Via al Sasso, 5-minute walk from Piazza Grande. Website: funicolarelocarno.ch.

activities.text: If the sun cooperates, ferries departing from Locarno's dock serve the Italian villages of Lake Maggiore. A way to change countries without a car for an afternoon. In August, boats are besieged: book the day before.

practical_info.tip: The dock is a 10-minute walk from Piazza Grande. Passport required to cross to the Italian side.

visits.text: From the terrace of Madonna del Sasso, Locarno shrinks to a rectangle of red roofs beside the lake. At just 355 meters, the view is total: lake, hills and Swiss Alps on clear days. The terrace remains open even outside Mass hours.

The Way of the Cross descending from the Madonna del Sasso sanctuary toward Locarno punctuates the slope with meditative chapels, each dedicated to a station of the Passion. The descent on foot takes 20 minutes. It's worth the detour even without particular devotion.

activities.text: Madonna del Sasso has the reputation of a tourist site, but its main chapel remains a place of authentic contemplation even in high season. The church is open from roughly 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. Best time: early morning, before the first groups arrive.

practical_info.tip: Funicular from downtown Locarno, departure Via al Sasso. The descent on foot via the Way of the Cross takes 20 min.

visits.text: Ascona's Piazza Motta runs directly along the shore of Lake Maggiore, its colorful arcades facing the water. Ascona is the lowest point in Switzerland (196 meters), which explains the palm trees and the mild climate. Arrive in late afternoon for aperitivo—it's the hour of the Ticinese, not yet of the tourists.

Kitsch, some say, but Monte Verità deserves better than that quick verdict. This hill above Ascona hosted from 1900 onward a colony of artists, anarchists and vegetarians led by Henri Oedenkoven and Ida Hofmann. The museum in Casa Anatta traces the utopia with seriousness. Allow 90 minutes; opt for a combined hill-and-museum ticket.

Ascona residents point to the Chiesa dei Santi Pietro e Paolo as the monument they're proudest of, despite its relative discretion. Baroque in architecture, it shelters remarkable frescoes often ignored by guidebooks. The door is often open during the day, free entry.

activities.text: Ascona's riva is a 5-minute walk from Piazza Motta: the lakeshore is set up for public swimming all summer. Hourly boat rentals are available along the waterfront. Lake Maggiore's water often exceeds 68°F in July and August.

practical_info.tip: Public swimming free from the riva, 5-minute walk from Piazza Motta.

activities.text: The aroma of coffee and prosecco mingles on the terraces late in the day: it's the rito dell'aperitivo, as serious as in neighboring Italy. The terraces of Piazza Motta are the liveliest. Come before 6 p.m. to get a table with a lake view.

practical_info.tip: For aperitivo, the terraces on Via Borgo, one street from the piazza, are generally less crowded.

visits.text: At the confluence of several alpine torrents, the town of Maggia marks the entrance to a major alpine valley. The Chiesa Madonna delle Grazie shelters Renaissance frescoes often overlooked by guidebooks. Free parking at the village entrance, starting point for backcountry hikes.

The road climbing the Vallemaggia seems ordinary, then it pivots: from Ponte Brolla onward, narrow granite gorges and stone villages emerge around every bend. The Vallemaggia is the largest valley in Italian-speaking Switzerland. The gorges between Ponte Brolla and the town of Maggia can be explored in a few roadside stops on the way up.

activities.text: The marked trail that follows the Maggia upstream offers several options from one hour to a half-day depending on available time. The river is accessible at numerous points for a swimming break. Prefer hiking shoes—the path can be slippery after rain.

practical_info.tip: Trail maps available at the tourist office in Palazzo Franzoni in Cevio.

visits.text: Vallemaggia residents call Cevio their 'capital' with a smile: yet this town is indeed the administrative seat of Ticino's largest valley. The central Piazza and Palazzo Franzoni set the village tone. Ideal for an hour's break between two valley segments.

Palazzo Franzoni, a historic building converted into a museum, traces daily life and the history of seasonal emigration from Vallemaggia families. The exhibitions on months spent working outside Ticino are particularly moving. Open June to October; check hours.

activities.text: The Sentiero di pietra, a paved path linking central Cevio to historic grotti, allows you to observe the valley's architecture in a 20-minute walk. Cheese and wine were once stored here in the natural coolness of the rock. Free, accessible in all seasons.

practical_info.tip: The Sentiero di pietra starts from Cevio's central Piazza. Free, accessible year-round.

seo.h1_title: Secret Ticino, 3 days: Bellinzona, Verzasca and Vallemaggia
seo.intro_paragraph: Bellinzona surprises with its three medieval castles listed as UNESCO World Heritage in 2000: a beginning as concrete as it is unexpected. From there, this 3-day road trip climbs toward the Verzasca with its emerald waters and the Vallemaggia, the largest valley in Italian-speaking Switzerland, before ending on the Mediterranean lakefront of Ascona, Switzerland's lowest point at 196 meters.
seo.seo_hook: Three UNESCO castles in Bellinzona, swims in the emerald Verzasca and granite villages in the Vallemaggia: the lesser-known Ticino in 3 days.
featured_list: Bellinzona: three medieval castles inscribed on UNESCO's list in 2000
Valle Verzasca: emerald water and Ponte dei Salti, a 17th-century bridge
Vallemaggia: granite gorges and stone villages off the tourist track
Ascona: Piazza Motta on the lake and Monte Verità, a utopia founded in 1900
